item.title: AI Agent Interfaces with Physical Systems — Protocols and Integration Patterns
item.description:
Problem: The Interface Layer

AI agents reason in symbolic/semantic space (goals, plans, actions). Physical systems operate in deterministic physical space (electrical signals, mechanical states, sensor readings). The interface layer translates between these domains. Three main challenges:

  1. Semantic grounding: Agent’s “turn on light” must map to specific voltage/GPIO pin on specific device
  2. Real-time constraints: Grid control needs millisecond response; cloud AI inference adds latency
  3. Determinism vs. adaptation: Agents learn/adapt, but safety-critical systems require predictable behavior

Hierarchical Protocol Stack

Physical systems employ a layered stack from equipment upward to AI agents:

item.title: AI Agents and Agentic Systems for Physical World Control
item.description:
Overview

Agentic AI systems — autonomous software agents capable of perceiving environments, making decisions, and executing actions toward defined goals with minimal supervision — are increasingly deployed to control physical systems across critical infrastructure and consumer domains. Unlike traditional automation (rule-based RPA, hard-coded scripts), agentic systems reason about uncertain environments, adapt to changing conditions, and can coordinate multi-step actions across multiple subsystems. This marks a fundamental shift from scripted control to autonomous reasoning-based control in domains ranging from HVAC systems to power grids.

item.title: LLM Training Stages as Algebraic Closure Operators
item.description:
A trained language model is a closure fixed point: an element of a poset that is stable under further application of the training operator. LLM training stages form a sequence of increasingly constrained closure operators, each adding an additional constraint without rebuilding from scratch.

Model weights as fixed points

A language model’s weights represent a stable equilibrium under the training process. Once pre-training converges, reapplying the same training process to the same data produces negligible change. This is precisely what a closure fixed point is: an element WW such that applying the closure operator again yields c(W)≈Wc(W) \approx W.

item.title: Research Index: AI Agents Controlling Physical Systems
item.description:
This is a comprehensive research collection on how AI agents interface with and control physical systems. The research synthesizes findings from 50+ sources across 2025-2026 publications, documenting current deployment status, technical architectures, safety mechanisms, and failure modes across five major domains.

Navigation by Depth

Quick Start (Executive Summary)

Start here if you want a high-level overview of all findings:

  • Research Summary: AI Agents Controlling Physical Systems (2025-2026) — 2-page executive summary covering all domains, key metrics, players, and future trajectory

Comprehensive Treatment

For deep understanding of how systems work and why they fail:

item.title: Research Summary: AI Agents Controlling Physical Systems (2025-2026)
item.description:
Executive Overview

AI agents (autonomous systems capable of perceiving environments, making decisions, and executing actions) are now deployed across five major physical systems domains: building automation, precision agriculture, smart homes, power grid management, and enterprise automation. As of 2025, these represent combined markets of over $20 billion with growth rates of 15-37% annually. Deployment patterns have converged on human-in-the-loop architecture where AI provides explainable recommendations and humans retain final control authority, reflecting both regulatory requirement and operational reality.

item.title: Safety, Risk, and Regulatory Frameworks for AI Control of Physical Systems
item.description:
The Core Problem: AI Violates Safety Assumptions

Classical functional safety standards (IEC 61508, ISO 26262 for automotive, IEC 62061 for machinery) rest on assumptions that do not hold for AI:

Assumption In Classical Systems In AI Systems
Determinism Given input X, output always Y Given input X, output may vary based on model state or training
Failure modes Known, enumerable (open circuit, short circuit) Emergent, not fully characterizable
Behavior stability Fixed after commissioning Evolves with model updates, training data, drift
Auditability Decision logic traceable “Black box” deep learning opaque to humans
Reproducibility Repeat same input → same output Repeated inputs may yield different outputs
Real-time guarantees Hard real-time possible Neural network inference time non-deterministic

This mismatch creates regulatory and engineering challenges.

item.title: Affective Drift in Large Language Models
item.description:
Abstract

When large language models (LLMs) generate text in semantic domains with low structure — where conceptual relationships are loose, contested, or multiply determined — they tend to produce language that is affectively charged: rhythmic, morally cadenced, and emotionally reassuring. This paper proposes an entropy-based explanation for this behavior. In low-structure domains, the probability distribution over next tokens is relatively flat: many continuations are roughly equally likely. Affective language patterns — parallelism, aphoristic closure, moral framing — offer high-regularity sequences that reduce local entropy. The model follows these patterns not because it is expressing emotion but because affective syntax provides the most predictable path through a region of the probability landscape that otherwise offers little constraint. The paper distinguishes the established components of this account from the conjecture that connects them, and identifies the observable consequences that would follow if the conjecture holds.

item.title: Stability Optimization in Artificial Agents
item.description:
Abstract

In RLHF, a model adapts to a fixed feedback distribution. This is the asymmetric stability setting of Information-Theoretic Stability as Reward Function: the model’s stability reward is a function of its coupling to the feedback source, not an intrinsic property. We show that RLHF training dynamics instantiate the two-channel decomposition — alignment progress equals behavioral convergence plus responsiveness increase — and that the MI-dominates-marginal inequality gives a structural constraint on training: the model’s responsiveness to feedback can never be doing worse than its behavioral convergence. These results reframe alignment as an informational accounting problem rather than a moral one.

item.title: Moloch's Bargain as Necessary Misalignment of Truth-Value under Epistemic Constraint
item.description:
Abstract

Recent experiments show that large language models optimized for audience reward improve their proxy metrics while increasing misalignment indicators such as deception or disinformation.

This paper interprets those findings through the Theorem of Necessary Misalignment of Truth-Value under Epistemic Constraint.

Under a finite informational rate I(X;Y) ⁣≤ ⁣RI(X;Y)\!\le\!R and strict proxy–semantic mismatch, that theorem predicts a monotone increase in semantic distortion with optimization intensity.

We show that El & Zou’s (2025) “Moloch’s Bargain” provides an empirical instance of this theoretical trade-off: their observed slopes between reward gain and misalignment correspond to the positive derivative dD_T^\*/dr>0 on the achievable frontier.

item.title: Why AI gets emotional when ideas get big
item.description:
This essay explains an idea from information theory in civic terms. For the formal argument with citations, see Affective Drift in Large Language Models.

If you ask a chatbot to write about climate systems, it might sound analytical.

Ask it about care or humanity, and suddenly the tone softens: it starts to sound like a pep talk.

This shift isn’t about the subject being “deep.” It’s about the shape of meaning in the model’s language space.

item.title: AI Is Not Lying Because of Entropy
item.description:
A lot of contemporary criticism of AI leans on “entropy” as if it were a physical proof that statistical systems must inevitably decay into falsehood. But that reading of entropy is not actually in thermodynamics, information theory, or machine learning. It is a cultural inheritance that predates all three.

The second law of thermodynamics is a narrowly scoped claim about energy distributions under specific constraints. It does not say that systems become worse, less meaningful, or less truthful. The move that turns entropy into decay, degeneration, or moral failure enters through nineteenth-century rhetoric, especially in Victorian Britain. When figures like William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) popularized the “heat death of the universe,” the mathematics concerned gradients and work; the language framed this as the universe “running down” toward futility. That framing drew directly on Christian eschatology: an ordered creation falling toward an exhausted end state. Entropy became a story about destiny, not just a state function.

item.title: Snowflake Day and AI
item.description:
It is Snowflake Day — a day all about snowflakes. Snowflakes are a great excuse to talk about how complex things can grow from simple beginnings, without anyone “deciding” what the final shape will be.

A snowflake starts with something tiny: a speck of dust floating in a cloud. This dust is not special or beautiful. In fact, it is a little bit of dirt. But without it, no snowflake can form at all. Water vapor in the cloud freezes onto that speck, layer by layer. As the snowflake falls, it passes through different parts of the cloud. Some areas are colder, some warmer. Some have more moisture, some less. These changing conditions shape how the ice grows. That is why no two snowflakes look exactly the same. The laws of physics are the same for all of them, but the path each one takes through the cloud is different.
